--- title: BMAD Synergy description: How SKF workflows pair with BMAD CORE phases and optional modules (BMM, TEA, BMB, GDS, CIS) — integration patterns, entry points, and artifact flow --- # Using SKF with BMAD This page is for people already using [BMAD](https://docs.bmad-method.org/) who want to know where SKF workflows plug in. It assumes you know what BMM phases are, what TEA does, and what a BMAD module is. If any of that is new, start with the [BMAD docs](https://docs.bmad-method.org/) and then come back. If you're new to SKF itself, read [Getting Started](../getting-started/) first. The rest of this page assumes you have SKF installed and have run `@Ferris SF`. --- ## Launcher Skills vs Content Skills A BMAD project that also uses SKF ends up with two different kinds of `SKILL.md` files living in the same platform skills directory (`.claude/skills/` for Claude Code, `.cursor/skills/` for Cursor, `.agents/skills/` for Copilot/Codex and similar). They look similar. They are not the same thing. This is the single most durable point of confusion, so get it straight up front. | | BMAD launcher skill | SKF content skill | |---|---|---| | **Created by** | `npx bmad-method install` (when you pick a module) | `@Ferris CS` / `QS` / `SS` | | **File contains** | A thin wrapper that loads a BMAD workflow, agent, or task | The instructions themselves, with citations to real source code | | **Updates when** | You reinstall or upgrade BMAD | You re-run SKF compilation against a new upstream version | | **Provenance** | Points to a BMAD workflow file inside `_bmad/` | Points to upstream repo commits, files, and line ranges | | **Example** | `bmad-create-prd/SKILL.md` loads a PRD workflow | `hono-4.6.0/SKILL.md` contains verified Hono API signatures | > BMAD skills *launch workflows*. SKF skills *are the workflows' output, frozen with citations*. Both coexist in the same platform skills directory on purpose. When a BMAD agent runs a workflow, that workflow can consult SKF content skills for verified API knowledge. The two kinds of skills compose — they don't compete. --- ## SKF and BMM: Phase-by-Phase Playbook BMM is BMAD's core [4-phase workflow](https://docs.bmad-method.org/) (Analysis → Planning → Solutioning → Implementation). SKF has five concrete entry points across those phases. The diagram below shows the end-to-end picture; the subsections that follow give the trigger, command, and artifact flow for each phase. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[BMM Phase 1: Analysis
product-brief · research] -.->|unfamiliar deps| AN[SKF: Analyze Source] AN --> BS[SKF: Brief Skill] BS -.->|risk register| P1 P1 --> P2[BMM Phase 2: Planning
create-prd] P2 -.->|uncertain API| QS[SKF: Quick Skill] QS -.->|verified API ref| P2 P2 --> P3[BMM Phase 3: Solutioning
create-architecture] P3 -.->|declared stack| VS[SKF: Verify Stack] VS --> RA[SKF: Refine Architecture] RA -.->|refined arch| P3b[BMM: check-implementation-readiness] P3 --> P3b P3b --> P4[BMM Phase 4: Implementation
create-story · dev-story] P4 -.->|story libs| CS[SKF: Create Skill / Stack Skill] CS -.->|verified skill context| P4 P4 --> RETRO[BMM: retrospective] RETRO -.->|API confusion found| US[SKF: Update Skill] US -.->|patched skill| P4 ``` ### Phase 1 — Analysis **Trigger:** A brownfield repo or an unfamiliar third-party dependency surfaces during `product-brief` or `research`. The team can't answer "what does this library actually expose?" from training data. **SKF command:** `@Ferris AN` on the repo, then `@Ferris BS` to scope each priority library. **What flows back:** Recommended skill boundaries, an analysis report of the discovered units, and one skill-brief per library that's ready to compile later. The scoping data is what PMs typically feed into their own risk register. **Why now, not later:** Catching surprise libraries during Analysis keeps the PRD honest. Discovering the same unknowns during Implementation forces course corrections that a two-paragraph risk entry could have prevented. ### Phase 2 — Planning **Trigger:** The PRD draft references an API and you realize nobody on the team is 100% sure how it behaves. **SKF command:** `@Ferris QS ` — no brief needed. **What flows back:** A verified skill you can cite directly in acceptance criteria. PM and architect read the same source. **Why now, not later:** Quick Skill is cheap insurance. It takes under a minute and prevents a whole class of "actually that function doesn't exist" moments during story writing. ### Phase 3 — Solutioning This is the highest-value integration. BMM's architect agent works from assumptions about the declared stack; SKF is how those assumptions become evidence-backed before the team commits to an implementation readiness check. **Trigger:** Architecture draft exists, `check-implementation-readiness` hasn't run yet. **SKF commands:** `@Ferris QS ` per declared dependency, then `@Ferris VS`, then `@Ferris RA` on any gaps or failures. **What flows back:** A pass/fail feasibility report per component, version-pinned evidence for every claim, and a refined architecture document with verified API signatures filled in at the callout points. **Why now, not later:** Running VS after Implementation has started means your stories are already built on an unverified foundation. The loop below is designed to iterate cheaply *before* code gets written. ```mermaid flowchart TD ARCH[BMM: create-architecture draft] --> GEN["SKF: Create Skill | Quick Skill
(per declared dependency)"] GEN --> VS[SKF: Verify Stack] VS -->|pass| READY[BMM: check-implementation-readiness] VS -->|fail / gaps| RA[SKF: Refine Architecture] RA -.->|refined draft| VS RA --> READY ``` The "Pre-Code Architecture Verification — Greenfield Confidence" scenario in [Examples](../examples/) walks through a concrete case of this loop. ### Phase 4 — Implementation Two distinct triggers fire during Implementation, one at the start of each story and one after each retrospective. **Trigger A (before `create-story`):** The story touches a library whose API isn't already in a content skill. **SKF command:** `@Ferris CS` for a single library, or `@Ferris SS` when the story spans several dependencies. **What flows back:** A verified content skill the `dev-story` workflow can consult during implementation — no training-data guessing about function signatures. **Trigger B (after `retrospective`):** The retro flagged something like "we kept getting API X wrong this sprint." **SKF command:** `@Ferris US` on the affected skill. **What flows back:** A patched skill with the newly-discovered edge cases captured — `[MANUAL]` sections preserved so human annotations aren't overwritten. Next sprint's stories consume the updated skill automatically. This retrospective → update loop is the pattern that [Scenario A in Examples](../examples/#scenario-a-greenfield--bmm-integration) sketches for one project; it generalizes to any BMM project that runs more than a few sprints. --- ## SKF with Optional BMAD Modules BMAD ships several [optional modules](https://docs.bmad-method.org/). Synergy with SKF ranges from very high (TEA) to none at all (CIS). This section is honest about both. ### TEA — Test Architect TEA produces structured test strategies and release gates. SKF produces the verified skills TEA's workflows need when the test target is a library they don't fully know. ```mermaid flowchart TD TD[TEA: Test Design] -.->|test lib unknown| CS[SKF: Create Skill
on Playwright/Vitest/Pact] CS --> AUTO[TEA: Automate / ATDD] AUTO --> GATE[TEA: Release Gate] GATE -.->|drift check| AS[SKF: Audit Skill] AS -->|no drift| GATE AS -.->|drift found| US[SKF: Update Skill] US -.->|refreshed skill| GATE ``` Two concrete integrations: - **Before Test Design / ATDD / Automate / Framework Scaffolding** — run `@Ferris CS` on whichever test library the strategy depends on (Playwright, Vitest, Pact, etc.). TEA's test-authoring agents then work against verified API surfaces instead of training-data approximations. - **Before Release Gate** — run `@Ferris AS` on the skills the gate cites. If the skill has drifted from the current source, the drift report itself becomes evidence the gate can act on, and `@Ferris US` closes the loop. ### BMB — BMad Builder BMB authors extend BMAD with new agents, workflows, or entire modules. When a new module depends on third-party libraries, ship a verified companion skill alongside it: - During `module-builder`, run `@Ferris SS` on the module's declared stack. The resulting stack skill becomes part of the module's distribution — downstream users get both the BMAD launcher skills (BMB's output) and the SKF content skills (your companion artifact) with one install. ### GDS — Game Dev Studio Narrow synergy. GDS covers GDD authoring, narrative design, and engine-specific guidance for 21+ game types, and most of that is conceptual work with no code to verify. The exception is when the GDD commits to a concrete engine SDK (Bevy, Godot-Rust, Unity DOTS): - Once the engine binding is pinned, run `@Ferris CS` on it. The implementation team then has verified bindings to work against. For narrative, character design, world-building, or genre research — no synergy. SKF has nothing to offer the creative side of GDS. ### CIS — Creative Intelligence Suite No direct synergy. CIS covers ideation, brainstorming, innovation strategy, and storytelling — phases with no code to verify. SKF's whole value proposition is turning code and docs into evidence-backed skills, and CIS operates upstream of any of that. Use CIS for the creative work, then bring SKF in once you're producing concrete technical artifacts. --- ## Delivery and Lifecycle in a BMAD Project SKF's `@Ferris EX` workflow is the **sole writer** to `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules`. No other SKF workflow touches those files. See [How It Works](../how-it-works/) for the architectural rationale. This matters specifically in a BMAD project: you may have multiple BMAD modules, each with its own launcher skills, plus SKF content skills, all trying to contribute context. The write-guard means only verified, tested SKF skills ever reach an agent's passive context — nothing half-baked sneaks in. `@Ferris EX` injects managed sections that coexist cleanly with whatever BMAD's installer wrote in the same files. For long-running BMAD projects, `@Ferris RS` (rename) and `@Ferris DS` (drop) keep the skill inventory clean as libraries get swapped, versions get deprecated, or naming conventions evolve across sprints. --- ## Where to Go Next - [BMAD docs](https://docs.bmad-method.org/) — canonical reference for BMM phases, TEA workflows, BMB / GDS / CIS details, and the full module list - [Workflows](../workflows/) — complete SKF workflow reference with commands and connection diagrams - [Examples](../examples/) — concrete scenarios including the BMM retrospective loop and greenfield architecture verification

`@Ferris EX` is the **only workflow that introduces new skill context** into `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules`. Create-skill and update-skill produce draft artifacts that never touch those files directly — nothing reaches an agent's passive context until it has been through the EX gate. See [How It Works](../how-it-works/) for the architectural rationale.

This matters specifically in a BMAD project: you may have multiple BMAD modules, each with its own launcher skills, plus SKF content skills, all trying to contribute context. The write-guard means only verified, tested SKF skills ever reach an agent's passive context — nothing half-baked sneaks in. `@Ferris EX` injects managed sections that coexist cleanly with whatever BMAD's installer wrote in the same files.

For long-running BMAD projects, `@Ferris RS` (rename) and `@Ferris DS` (drop) keep the skill inventory clean as libraries get swapped, versions get deprecated, or naming conventions evolve across sprints. Both *rebuild* the existing managed sections in `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules` so references stay consistent after a rename or drop — they never inject previously-unpublished content, so the EX gate still governs what initially enters those files.

The implementation team then has verified bindings to work against. For narrative, character design, world-building, or genre research — no synergy. SKF has nothing to offer the creative side of GDS. ### CIS — Creative Intelligence Suite No direct synergy. CIS covers ideation, brainstorming, innovation strategy, and storytelling — phases with no code to verify. SKF's whole value proposition is turning code and docs into evidence-backed skills, and CIS operates upstream of any of that. Use CIS for the creative work, then bring SKF in once you're producing concrete technical artifacts. --- ## Delivery and Lifecycle in a BMAD Project SKF's `@Ferris EX` workflow is the **sole writer** to `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules`. No other SKF workflow touches those files. See [How It Works](../how-it-works/) for the architectural rationale. This matters specifically in a BMAD project: you may have multiple BMAD modules, each with its own launcher skills, plus SKF content skills, all trying to contribute context. The write-guard means only verified, tested SKF skills ever reach an agent's passive context — nothing half-baked sneaks in. `@Ferris EX` injects managed sections that coexist cleanly with whatever BMAD's installer wrote in the same files. For long-running BMAD projects, `@Ferris RS` (rename) and `@Ferris DS` (drop) keep the skill inventory clean as libraries get swapped, versions get deprecated, or naming conventions evolve across sprints. --- ## Where to Go Next - [BMAD docs](https://docs.bmad-method.org/) — canonical reference for BMM phases, TEA workflows, BMB / GDS / CIS details, and the full module list - [Workflows](../workflows/) — complete SKF workflow reference with commands and connection diagrams - [Examples](../examples/) — concrete scenarios including the BMM retrospective loop and greenfield architecture verification

+`@Ferris EX` is the **only workflow that introduces new skill context** into `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules`. Create-skill and update-skill produce draft artifacts that never touch those files directly — nothing reaches an agent's passive context until it has been through the EX gate. See [How It Works](../how-it-works/) for the architectural rationale.

This matters specifically in a BMAD project: you may have multiple BMAD modules, each with its own launcher skills, plus SKF content skills, all trying to contribute context. The write-guard means only verified, tested SKF skills ever reach an agent's passive context — nothing half-baked sneaks in. `@Ferris EX` injects managed sections that coexist cleanly with whatever BMAD's installer wrote in the same files.

-For long-running BMAD projects, `@Ferris RS` (rename) and `@Ferris DS` (drop) keep the skill inventory clean as libraries get swapped, versions get deprecated, or naming conventions evolve across sprints.

+For long-running BMAD projects, `@Ferris RS` (rename) and `@Ferris DS` (drop) keep the skill inventory clean as libraries get swapped, versions get deprecated, or naming conventions evolve across sprints. Both *rebuild* the existing managed sections in `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules` so references stay consistent after a rename or drop — they never inject previously-unpublished content, so the EX gate still governs what initially enters those files.

14	## Launcher Skills vs Content Skills
15	
16	A BMAD project that also uses SKF ends up with two different kinds of `SKILL.md` files living in the same platform skills directory (`.claude/skills/` for Claude Code, `.cursor/skills/` for Cursor, `.agents/skills/` for Copilot/Codex and similar). They look similar. They are not the same thing. This is the single most durable point of confusion, so get it straight up front.
17	
18	| | BMAD launcher skill | SKF content skill |
19	|---|---|---|
20	| **Created by** | `npx bmad-method install` (when you pick a module) | `@Ferris CS` / `QS` / `SS` |
21	| **File contains** | A thin wrapper that loads a BMAD workflow, agent, or task | The instructions themselves, with citations to real source code |
22	| **Updates when** | You reinstall or upgrade BMAD | You re-run SKF compilation against a new upstream version |
23	| **Provenance** | Points to a BMAD workflow file inside `_bmad/` | Points to upstream repo commits, files, and line ranges |
24	| **Example** | `bmad-create-prd/SKILL.md` loads a PRD workflow | `hono-4.6.0/SKILL.md` contains verified Hono API signatures |
25	
26	> BMAD skills *launch workflows*. SKF skills *are the workflows' output, frozen with citations*. Both coexist in the same platform skills directory on purpose.
27	
28	When a BMAD agent runs a workflow, that workflow can consult SKF content skills for verified API knowledge. The two kinds of skills compose — they don't compete.
29	
30	---

file.content:
## Launcher Skills vs Content Skills

A BMAD project that also uses SKF ends up with two different kinds of `SKILL.md` files living in the same platform skills directory (`.claude/skills/` for Claude Code, `.cursor/skills/` for Cursor, `.agents/skills/` for Copilot/Codex and similar). They look similar. They are not the same thing. This is the single most durable point of confusion, so get it straight up front.

| | BMAD launcher skill | SKF content skill |
|---|---|---|
| **Created by** | `npx bmad-method install` (when you pick a module) | `@Ferris CS` / `QS` / `SS` |
| **File contains** | A thin wrapper that loads a BMAD workflow, agent, or task | The instructions themselves, with citations to real source code |
| **Updates when** | You reinstall or upgrade BMAD | You re-run SKF compilation against a new upstream version |
| **Provenance** | Points to a BMAD workflow file inside `_bmad/` | Points to upstream repo commits, files, and line ranges |
| **Example** | `bmad-create-prd/SKILL.md` loads a PRD workflow | `hono-4.6.0/SKILL.md` contains verified Hono API signatures |

> BMAD skills *launch workflows*. SKF skills *are the workflows' output, frozen with citations*. Both coexist in the same platform skills directory on purpose.

When a BMAD agent runs a workflow, that workflow can consult SKF content skills for verified API knowledge. The two kinds of skills compose — they don't compete.

---

## SKF and BMM: Phase-by-Phase Playbook

No direct synergy. CIS covers ideation, brainstorming, innovation strategy, and storytelling — phases with no code to verify. SKF's whole value proposition is turning code and docs into evidence-backed skills, and CIS operates upstream of any of that. Use CIS for the creative work, then bring SKF in once you're producing concrete technical artifacts.

---

## Delivery and Lifecycle in a BMAD Project

`@Ferris EX` is the **only workflow that introduces new skill context** into `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules`. Create-skill and update-skill produce draft artifacts that never touch those files directly — nothing reaches an agent's passive context until it has been through the EX gate. See [How It Works](../how-it-works/) for the architectural rationale.

This matters specifically in a BMAD project: you may have multiple BMAD modules, each with its own launcher skills, plus SKF content skills, all trying to contribute context. The write-guard means only verified, tested SKF skills ever reach an agent's passive context — nothing half-baked sneaks in. `@Ferris EX` injects managed sections that coexist cleanly with whatever BMAD's installer wrote in the same files.

For long-running BMAD projects, `@Ferris RS` (rename) and `@Ferris DS` (drop) keep the skill inventory clean as libraries get swapped, versions get deprecated, or naming conventions evolve across sprints. Both *rebuild* the existing managed sections in `CLAUDE.md`, `AGENTS.md`, and `.cursorrules` so references stay consistent after a rename or drop — they never inject previously-unpublished content, so the EX gate still governs what initially enters those files.

---

## Where to Go Next

content.text:
All 5 fixes applied and verified:

1. **HIGH — "sole writer" self-contradiction** (line 171, 175): Reworded to "only workflow that introduces new skill context". RS/DS now explicitly described as *rebuilding* existing managed sections, resolving the contradiction with the paragraph that mentions their platform context rebuild.
2. **MEDIUM — fabricated "unknowns" artifact** (line 67): Replaced with "analysis report of the discovered units", with a separate sentence noting teams *use* the scoping data for their risk register rather than implying SKF outputs a risk register itself.
3. **LOW — "Murat's agent"** (line 146): Now "TEA's test-authoring agents" — verifiable from TEA's purpose, not dependent on unverifiable maintainer knowledge.
4. **LOW — `.claude/skills/` as universal** (lines 16, 26): Now enumerates all three platform directories from `src/workflows/export-skill/data/managed-section-format.md`.
5. **LOW — "BMB's output = launcher skills"** (line 153): Reworded to "the BMAD module BMB built" — drops the unverifiable inference about BMB specifically producing launcher skills.

`docs/bmad-synergy.md` is new (untracked), so `git diff --stat` is empty by design — `git status` still shows it as `??`. The other 5 files' edits were not touched.

name: Manual Release

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version_bump:
        description: Version bump type
        required: true
        default: alpha
        type: choice
        options:
          - alpha
          - beta
          - patch
          - minor
          - major

permissions:
  contents: write
  packages: write

jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
          token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: npm
          registry-url: https://registry.npmjs.org

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Run tests and validation
        run: |
          npm test

      - name: Configure Git
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"

file.content:
name: Deploy Documentation

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
    paths:
      - "docs/**"
      - "src/modules/*/docs/**"
      - "website/**"
      - "tools/build-docs.js"
      - ".github/workflows/docs.yaml"
  workflow_dispatch:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pages: write
  id-token: write

concurrency:
  group: "pages"
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version-file: ".nvmrc"
          cache: "npm"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: npm ci

      - name: Install website dependencies
        run: npm ci --prefix website

      - name: Build documentation
        env:
          # Override site URL from GitHub repo variable if set
          # Otherwise, astro.config.mjs will compute from GITHUB_REPOSITORY
          SITE_URL: ${{ vars.SITE_URL }}
        run: npm run docs:build

      - name: Upload artifact
        uses: actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3
        with:
          path: build/site

  deploy:
    environment:
      name: github-pages
      url: ${{ steps.deployment.outputs.page_url }}
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: build
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        id: deployment
        uses: actions/deploy-pages@v4

content.text:
Yes, this is straightforward. There are two good approaches — they differ in what "synced with npm" means in practice:

## Option A — Client-side fetch from npm registry (literally live)

A small script in `Header.astro` calls `https://registry.npmjs.org/bmad-module-skill-forge/latest` on page load and renders the version. The npm registry supports CORS, so this just works.

- ✅ Always shows the actual npm-published version, even between docs rebuilds
- ✅ Zero CI/build changes needed
- ⚠️ Brief flash before the badge resolves (mitigated with a fallback value)
- ⚠️ Requires network; fails silently if blocked

## Option B — Build-time read from `package.json`

`astro.config.mjs` imports `package.json` version at build, passes it via environment/virtual module, and `Header.astro` renders it statically.

- ✅ No runtime fetch, no flash, no network dependency
- ✅ "Actually deployed on npm" because your release flow (`npm version patch && git push --follow-tags` in package.json:43, then `manual-release.yaml`) bumps `package.json` as the source of truth for npm
- ⚠️ **Gotcha:** `.github/workflows/docs.yaml:7-12` only triggers on `docs/**`, `website/**`, `src/modules/*/docs/**`, `tools/build-docs.js`. A root-level `package.json` version bump does **not** rebuild the docs site — so the header would lag until the next unrelated docs commit. Fix is a one-line addition: add `package.json` to the `paths:` trigger.

## My recommendation

**Option B + the docs.yaml trigger fix.** Reasons:

1. Your release flow already treats `package.json` as the canonical version — npm publishes from it and so does your git tag. Reading the same file closes the loop without introducing a new source of truth.
2. No client-side network dependency means the version is visible even on slow connections, in llms.txt, and in any SSR/prerendered HTML snapshot.
3. One-line CI tweak is cheaper than a 30-line client-fetch-with-fallback component.

The only reason to pick A is if you want the header to update between docs deploys (e.g. if a release publishes to npm but the docs site won't rebuild for hours). That scenario doesn't match your current setup since `manual-release.yaml` runs `npm test` and expects a fresh commit — the next docs trigger will typically follow closely.

Which do you want? I can implement either in roughly the same number of edits.

content.text: Path resolves correctly — `v0.8.4` will render from the root `package.json`. Let me also do a dry check to catch any `astro check` / TypeScript complaints about the Header component:

All changes applied. Summary:

**`website/src/components/Header.astro`**
- Frontmatter reads root `package.json` via `fileURLToPath(new URL('../../../package.json', import.meta.url))` — path resolution verified (resolves to `/home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/package.json` returning `0.8.4`).
- A pill-shaped badge `v0.8.4` renders next to `<SiteTitle />` inside `.title-wrapper`, linked to `https://www.npmjs.com/package/bmad-module-skill-forge/v/{version}` with `target="_blank" rel="noopener"` and an `aria-label` for screen readers.
- Scoped CSS uses Starlight's design tokens (`--sl-color-gray-5/6`, `--sl-text-xs`) so it follows theme changes automatically.

**`.github/workflows/docs.yaml`**
- Added `"package.json"` to the `paths:` trigger. Now a `npm version patch && git push --follow-tags` from your release script will auto-rebuild the docs site so the badge never lags behind what's on npm.

Nothing else was touched. No changes to `astro.config.mjs` — the component reads `package.json` directly, which is simpler than plumbing the value through a virtual module.

Test it locally with `npm run docs:dev` from the repo root when convenient.

message.content: I don't like that position. Is there a better place we could show that version tag?

content.text:
A few alternatives, with trade-offs:

1. **Right side of the header, before the GitHub icon** (inside `.right-group`)
   Most common pattern in docs sites (Astro, Vite, Vue). Groups the version with other "project meta" links. Clean, unobtrusive, pairs visually with the GitHub link.
   ⚠️ Hidden on mobile — the right group is `sl-hidden md:sl-flex`. Would need a companion addition to `MobileMenuFooter.astro` for mobile visibility.

2. **Inline with the social icons row** (inside `.social-icons`, before the GitHub icon)
   Tighter grouping with GitHub specifically — reads as "here's the repo, here's its current release". Same mobile caveat as #1.

3. **Footer only** (via `MobileMenuFooter.astro` on mobile + add a Footer component on desktop)
   Lowest prominence. Out of the way. Good if you consider the version reference info, not navigation.

4. **Under the tagline as a subtle subtitle** — Not really available without overriding the `SiteTitle` component itself, which is more invasive than you probably want.

**My recommendation:** Option 1. It matches what users already scan for (top-right, near repo links), survives theme/layout changes cleanly, and the mobile caveat is easily handled by mirroring it into `MobileMenuFooter.astro`. Visually it becomes one of three chips: `v0.8.4` · GitHub icon · theme toggle.

Want me to go with option 1 (including the mobile footer mirror), option 2, or something else?

input.content:
# Organize session work into 3 focused commits

## Context

This session produced three logically independent changes on the `dev` branch, plus five pre-session pending edits that all tie into the first change as cross-links. The user wants them grouped as three narrowly-scoped commits rather than a single mixed commit.

Why it matters:
- Recent history on `dev` follows a "one concern per commit" style (`docs:`, `feat:`, `fix:` prefixes, lowercase descriptions — see `git log --oneline -5`)
- The title-rename and the version-badge work should be reviewable in isolation
- `website/astro.config.mjs` contains hunks for **two different commits**, so a targeted split via `git add -p` is required — this is the only non-obvious mechanic in the plan

## Current pending changes (verified via `git status` + `git diff`)

| File | Status | Belongs to |
|---|---|---|
| `docs/bmad-synergy.md` | untracked (new) | Commit 1 |
| `docs/concepts.md` | modified | Commit 1 |
| `docs/examples.md` | modified | Commit 1 |
| `docs/how-it-works.md` | modified | Commit 1 |
| `docs/workflows.md` | modified | Commit 1 |
| `website/astro.config.mjs` | modified (**2 hunks**) | Sidebar hunk → Commit 1, title/alt hunk → Commit 2 |
| `website/src/components/Header.astro` | modified | Commit 3 |
| `website/src/components/MobileMenuFooter.astro` | modified | Commit 3 |
| `.github/workflows/docs.yaml` | modified | Commit 3 |

Verified hunks in `website/astro.config.mjs`:
- **Hunk @@ -40..48** — `title: 'Skill Forge (SKF)'` → `'Skill Forge'` and logo `alt` same rename → **Commit 2**
- **Hunk @@ -106..111** — adds `{ label: 'BMAD Synergy', slug: 'bmad-synergy' }` to the sidebar → **Commit 1**

---

## Commit 1 — `docs: add BMAD Synergy guide with cross-references`

**Scope:** Introduce the new BMAD integration guide and wire it into navigation and existing docs pages.

**Rationale:** The five pre-session pending doc edits all add cross-links pointing *into* `docs/bmad-synergy.md`, and the sidebar entry is how readers discover it. Shipping them together keeps the new page and all its entry points in a single atomic change — reviewers see a self-contained feature.

**Files:**
- `docs/bmad-synergy.md` (new file — reflects the 5 in-session accuracy fixes applied this session)
- `docs/concepts.md`
- `docs/examples.md`
- `docs/how-it-works.md`
- `docs/workflows.md`
- `website/astro.config.mjs` — **only the sidebar hunk** (lines ~106–111)

**Commands:**
```bash
git add docs/bmad-synergy.md docs/concepts.md docs/examples.md \
        docs/how-it-works.md docs/workflows.md
git add -p website/astro.config.mjs
# At the prompts:
#   - First hunk (title + alt rename @@ 40-48):  answer 'n'
#   - Second hunk (sidebar entry @@ 106-111):    answer 'y'

git diff --cached website/astro.config.mjs   # sanity check: only sidebar line is staged
git commit -m "docs: add BMAD Synergy guide with cross-references"
```

---

## Commit 2 — `docs: drop "(SKF)" suffix from site title and logo alt`

**Scope:** Branding simplification in the Starlight config. Zero functional impact.

**Files:**
- `website/astro.config.mjs` — the remaining hunk (title + logo alt)

**Commands:**
```bash
git add website/astro.config.mjs
git diff --cached website/astro.config.mjs   # should show ONLY the title + alt rename
git commit -m 'docs: drop "(SKF)" suffix from site title and logo alt'
```

---

## Commit 3 — `feat: show npm version badge in docs header`

**Scope:** Read `package.json` version at Astro build time, render it as a clickable pill badge in the desktop header's right group and in the mobile menu footer, linked to the npm version page. Extend the docs-deploy workflow to rebuild on `package.json` changes so the badge never lags behind a release.

**Rationale for grouping:** All three files are in service of one feature — the header component renders the badge, the mobile footer mirrors it for small screens, and the CI trigger ensures the displayed value stays truthful. Splitting further would leave the feature half-landed.

**Files:**
- `website/src/components/Header.astro` — frontmatter reads `../../../package.json`, renders `<a class="version-badge">` in `.right-group` before social icons; scoped CSS uses `--sl-color-gray-5/6` tokens
- `website/src/components/MobileMenuFooter.astro` — identical badge + CSS for the mobile menu footer
- `.github/workflows/docs.yaml` — adds `"package.json"` to the `paths:` trigger list

**Commands:**
```bash
git add website/src/components/Header.astro \
        website/src/components/MobileMenuFooter.astro \
        .github/workflows/docs.yaml
git commit -m "feat: show npm version badge in docs header"
```

---

## Verification

After each commit:
```bash
git log --oneline -1      # confirm message
git show --stat HEAD      # confirm file list matches the commit's scope
```

After all three commits:
```bash
git status                              # must be clean — nothing left staged or modified
git log --oneline -3                    # top 3 lines = the three new commits in order
git diff HEAD~3 HEAD --stat             # total = 9 files changed (the 9 from the original git status)
```

Optional end-to-end check (validates commit 3 doesn't break the site):
```bash
npm run --prefix website build          # Astro must resolve ../../../package.json and render v{version}
```

## Notes and constraints

- **Do not `git add .` or `git add -A`** at any point — commit 1 must leave the title/alt rename unstaged, and a blanket add would lose the hunk split.
- **Do not amend existing published commits** — all three are new commits on top of `dev`; `dev` is already 19 ahead of `origin/dev` but nothing here rewrites history.
- **Do not push** after committing — user has not asked for that. Stop at `git status` clean.
- Commit message prefixes (`docs:`, `feat:`) match the existing convention on `dev`; verified against `git log --oneline -5`.
- No `Co-Authored-By` footer in these messages unless the user explicitly wants one — the repo's existing commits don't use them either.
